posts.title: Flutter animations can be addictive
posts.content: I said I’d just add a simple transition and somehow ended up polishing micro-interactions for an hour. Worth it though.

posts.title: CI pipelines should fail loudly
posts.content: Updated a GitHub Actions workflow today so failures are clearer and easier to diagnose. Good logs save real time.

posts.title: Thunks feel less scary now
posts.content: Async logic looked messy at first, but once I understood pending, fulfilled, and rejected states, the pattern started to feel natural.

posts.title: Component composition over giant components
posts.content: Split one oversized page into smaller reusable pieces today and immediately felt less stressed. Cleaner code, easier testing.

posts.title: Spacing can make or break a UI
posts.content: No new features today, just tightened layout spacing and typography rhythm. The interface instantly looked more professional.

posts.title: Why I still like REST for learning
posts.content: GraphQL is cool, but for practicing data fetching patterns and client state flow, a simple REST API keeps the learning signal really clear.

posts.title: Testing reducers gave me more confidence
posts.content: Wrote a few reducer tests today and it made refactoring way less stressful. Fast feedback loops are underrated.

posts.title: Feature folders are winning me over
posts.content: Grouping slices, selectors, components, and hooks by feature instead of file type is making the project feel much easier to navigate.

posts.title: State design matters more than I thought
posts.content: A lot of bugs disappeared once I stopped stuffing everything into arrays and started being intentional about state shape.

posts.title: Understanding RTK Query Caching
posts.content: RTK Query automatically caches server responses and shares them across components. This reduces unnecessary network requests and makes state management much cleaner compared to traditional async thunk patterns.

posts.title: Why Redux Toolkit Feels Cleaner
posts.content: After switching from classic Redux boilerplate to Redux Toolkit, managing reducers, actions, and async logic became significantly easier. The developer experience is honestly much smoother.

posts.title: Optimistic Updates Are Amazing
posts.content: Implemented optimistic post creation and deletion today. Seeing the UI update instantly before the server responds makes the application feel much faster and more responsive.

posts.title: Reducers Must Stay Predictable
posts.content: One of the biggest lessons from learning Redux is understanding predictable state updates. Pure reducers make debugging easier because the same action always produces the same state transition.

posts.title: Migrating From Thunks to RTK Query
posts.content: Started migrating my CRUD operations from createAsyncThunk to RTK Query. The amount of boilerplate removed is honestly impressive.

posts.title: Memoized Selectors Explained
posts.content: Selectors using filter or map can return new references on every render. Memoizing them with createSelector helps prevent unnecessary re-renders and improves performance.

posts.title: Learning Cache Invalidation
posts.content: RTK Query tags and invalidation finally clicked for me today. Mutations can invalidate cached queries and automatically trigger fresh data fetching.

posts.title: The Difference Between Client and Server State
posts.content: RTK Query handles server state beautifully, while Redux slices are still useful for UI state like modals, toasts, and theme preferences.

posts.title: Immer Makes Redux Mutations Feel Natural
posts.content: Redux Toolkit uses Immer under the hood, which allows writing reducers with mutation-like syntax while still preserving immutability internally.

posts.title: RTK Query Mutation Hooks
posts.content: Mutation hooks in RTK Query return both a trigger function and mutation state. Using unwrap() makes async handling feel very similar to createAsyncThunk.

posts.title: Leaving rejectWithValue Behind
posts.content: One thing I noticed after migrating to RTK Query is that I no longer need manual rejectWithValue handling for most server requests. RTK Query automatically exposes loading, error, and success states through generated hooks.

posts.title: No More extraReducers Boilerplate
posts.content: With createAsyncThunk, I had to handle pending, fulfilled, and rejected cases manually inside extraReducers. RTK Query abstracts most of that away and gives cleaner server-state management out of the box.

posts.title: RTK Query vs createAsyncThunk
posts.content: createAsyncThunk gave me full control over async workflows using tools like getState, rejectWithValue, and unwrap. RTK Query feels more declarative because fetching, caching, and invalidation are built directly into the API layer.

posts.title: Understanding RTK Query Cache Tags
posts.content: Learning providesTags and invalidatesTags made me understand how RTK Query keeps server data synchronized automatically. It replaces a lot of the manual refetch logic I previously wrote with thunks.

posts.title: Generated Hooks Feel Like Magic
posts.content: One of my favorite RTK Query features so far is automatic hook generation. Defining endpoints instantly gives hooks like useGetPostsQuery and useCreatePostMutation without writing custom selectors or dispatch logic.

posts.title: Server State Should Not Feel Manual
posts.content: After working with RTK Query, managing server state manually with slices and extraReducers now feels much more verbose. RTK Query centralizes API logic and simplifies component data fetching significantly.
